item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As the wiki inherently cannot use images licensed under CC BY-ND 2.0, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But he doesn't raise the "red flags" that a serial killer does. I thought that whole psychoanalysis part hinted that there was more of a reason to his killings, and it wasn't total nonsense. It also felt like maybe he didn't want her to come back over and over. I know they said that he had something to do with it, but it really feels…

Hmm…

It feels like it is an end to a mean, not a casual pastime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely well-constructed, but it ultimately left me cold. I don't care for serial killers, topically, and after the initial reveal of Everret's relationship with Mildred, the article just kept reiterating 1) he kills Mildreds 2) he is a creepy dude. The diary entry, while decently done, doesn't add anything to the story, beyond "he is good at luring Mildreds to their grisly deaths."

The bit about Everret being reborn at the end was a good twist, but not ultimately enough to save the subject matter. -1

Also, regarding the brainy French person being Dr. Durand, this seems so counter to the goals and motivations of Durand that I can't really imagine them being the same character. My thought for some reason that "Dr. Anne Juda" was some sort of scramble of "d'Anjou," a French surname (and also a kind of pear), although that might just be residual Crusader Kings II logic. As per And Again, it is almost certainly is Dr. Durand. I feel like this is a kind of nonsensical addition, even with the sort-of justification in And Again. Downvote remains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think we got ourselves a complete monster in the form of Mr. Everett, a sociopathic serial killer who doesn't have any emotional bonds to anyone besides having this obsession with girls having a specific genetic makeup. What makes me not so sure about this is that he could be part of something much larger. Either that or he sold something of his just to sate his desire for killing the same girl over and over again.

Too bad the author's not here anymore to clarify all this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, thought of 1646, but this has a very different feel to it. I particularly love that last thing. Not only is it a good addendum, but the whole "flashing message" setup is really cool! I've never seen it before! And I have no idea how I ever missed this one.

item.title: Easter Egg
item.content:encoded:
descriptions of his "egghead pal from France" as his nonexistant accomplice

That's Durand from SCP-186, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That message at the end really solidifies the immersive effect this has already. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Include into data file that review by SCP-2888 research personnel indicates that all furnishings in the house recovered by MTF Beta-88 were removed from the homes of SCP-2888 instances, presumably by Mr. Everett.

I may be wrong, but I think there's a grammar or syntax error here, at least according to Microsoft Word. This was suggested:

[…] indicates that Mr. Everett removed all furnishings in the house recovered by MTF Beta-88 from the homes of SCP-2888 instances, presumably.

However you may want to keep the original version as it reveals the individual at the end of the sentence. Your choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you search this SCP on google it has "Did you mean: SPC-2888" :|

WAT

EDIT: Can someone explain this skip? I'm really confused about it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, that's my problem with it, too. It states that the births started occurring in 1957, but with a grown working woman meeting up in 1963, the times are off.

However, I did consider the idea that this has been going on far longer, and that Mildred wasn't even the first. Finishing up with the new Marshall at the end, this could have been going on for centuries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That diary entry doesn't sound like it was written by a 5-year-old… as that's the maximum age she could be at that date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy, chilling, and knows the exact right amount of information to give out. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be one of my new favorite articles. I love the tone, very spooky, and the "incoming message" at the end is spectacular. Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a lot of SCP-1646 (the central conceit, of somebody killing one person over and over again, is repeated), but goes in a completely different direction. I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She didn't do anything. It's just what gets his goat. That's what makes serial killers, both real and fictional, so damn creepy; they do what they do either purely for sport, or out of habit, never because their victims "deserve" it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What did she *do* to make him hate her so much? Also, why do I have the feeling that this is not the first cycle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was gonna make a long spiel about why I like this, but I think it's sufficient to say this is unsettling in all the right ways. GG, Kalinin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, it was fascinating enough just as it was, then I saw the message at the bottom and my fucking head exploded!
A key part of Foundation immersion is making the reader feel like they're someone with clearance who's actually reading the files, but I've never seen an entry where a message pops up and actually speaks to me as if I were a researcher!
Take all of my upvote and fifty more besides!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: holy shieet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, normally that glitch is just kinda funny. But given that it's this article, and sometimes people say "I wish I could upvote twice"?

I propose we let the double-upvote stand for creepy easter egg purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, schifter…you have a double-upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you care to share the hints for those of us still scratching our heads? I really liked it but can't see anything that would hint at an actual cause.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Story? I didn't get a feel for that much of a story in this. It's a guy who cloned a woman so he could kill her over and over again possibly due to some sort of crazy love he had for her.
Oh, and he might have tried to insulate his house with her bodies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wish there was more evidence as to how he cloned her.

There's some hints as to one possible interpretation of how it might have happened. They're buried pretty deep, though, as it's ultimately unimportant to the story as I imagine it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the slow burn on this one, and its dedication to a dynamic narrative. I particularly like the confirmation of anomalous status early in the description, as well as the minor format screw at the end. It was also nice to see a "last note" that wasn't a pat explanation for the entirety of the skip and a format screw that wasn't empty showboating. It was format alteration serving and extending the mystery of the narrative at its close rather than just resolving it in a few hasty lines.

I did feel that some editing could be done. For instance, this section:

Examination of materials recovered after the detainment of Mr. Everett confirmed this relationship. While the mechanism by which SCP-2888 occurs remains unknown, research at the time, combined with witness statements and other collected evidence, indicated that Mr. Everett at least partially influenced the commencement of 38 births of specimens genetically identical to Ms. Reckart.

Seems awkwardly constructed and worded to me. It could be simplified:

Materials recovered following Mr. Everett's detention confirmed this relationship. While the mechanism by which SCP-2888 occurs remains unknown, all research indicates Mr. Everatt at least partially influenced 38 successful live births of specimens genetically identical to Ms. Reckart.

This is, of course, merely a suggestion, but it is emblematic of the minor editing I think could be done throughout the work to make it even more effective.

I also balked slightly at the intake documentation. We establish through the description that this individual is intelligent and apparently believes in feigning madness as a reliable legal defense. Much of his preparation for his "work" is done to evade (successfully) legal authority. While this is interesting, (he does not recognize he is in thoroughly extralegal territory), the psych intake largely seems to be sand from which we are to sift the one moment of honesty regarding Everett's monstrous justifications. The extensive documentation explaining that he is attempting to avoid culpability and that he is a nonstandard killer is not necessary, in my opinion—these points are made clear through the description and from the monomaniacal and anomalous nature of the crime.

With regard to the "how he did it" angle? I for one am not unsettled by a lack of explanation. How is irrelevant. How is a MacGuffin. Madness like Everett's found a way to be sated. That's all we need know on the topic, in my opinion.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I'm gonna disagree with Sound Chaser here and say that, in my opinion, this WAS mindblowing. I love how in-detail it felt, like it was an actual official government document. I love the nonsensical responses that Mr. Everett gave. I love the excerpt from the victim's diary. And I especially love the "You have one new message" tidbit at the bottom. When I read that the new subject was a match for Everett instead of Reckart, I had a "HOLY SHIT" moment.

Glorious. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er… I don't know how that became a reply. I could have sworn I clicked on New Post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wish there was more evidence as to how he cloned her. It's kinda frustrating going through this long article only to find out there's nothing on how he did all this.

Oh, and the section about his house feels like it could have been shortened, a lot. The same with the psych evaluation. The journal entry also feels a bit long-winded. But this could just be me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really enjoyable. The tone is perfect, the concept is VERY intriguing, and the way you slowly revealed the story was fun. I'm impressed with this one Kal.

Whole-hearted +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like how different this is. Without needing to be a format screw or nuffin' you've made something that doesn't quite feel like much else here, the best example of a 'living document' style of article that we don't see very often. I like this very much. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher's Note: While Mr. Marshall kept possession of numerous artifacts

Should that be "Mr. Everett"?

Overall, this wasn't mindblowing, but I did like it. Everett's single-minded determination and the house were, to me, the best parts of it.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
First pic is used with the guidance of the Image Team and is probably public domain. Someone with more knowledge than I please free to elaborate if necessary.

Second image was taken by flickr account user gb_packards (may be accessed here), and is unaltered per the terms of the CC BY-ND 2.0 licensing agreement.

Thanks to djkaktus and Devereaux for their help with sourcing and messing with the images.

EDIT: Also, thanks to Crayne for the coding at the end.
